channel.title: Nature Communications
channel.description:
Nature Communications is an open access, multidisciplinary journal dedicated to publishing high-quality research in all areas of the biological, health, physical, chemical and Earth sciences. Papers published by the journal aim to represent important advances of significance to specialists within each field.

We are committed to providing an efficient service for both authors and readers. Our team of independent editors make rapid and fair publication decisions. Prompt dissemination of accepted papers to a wide readership and beyond is achieved through a programme of continuous online publication.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: Competition between protein-RNA clustering and phase separation drives re-entrant phase behavior of hnRNPA1
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-71939-2

RNA levels control phase separation of RNA-binding proteins in cells. Here, the authors show that high RNA concentrations suppress hnRNPA1 phase separation by inducing the formation of nano-sized protein-RNA clusters in the dilute phase, which convert to amyloid fibrils more slowly than condensates.
item.dc:title: Competition between protein-RNA clustering and phase separation drives re-entrant phase behavior of hnRNPA1
item.dc:creator: Katarzyna Makasewicz, Chiara Morelli, Tommaso Guida, Lenka Faltova, Paolo Arosio
Chiara Morelli
Tommaso Guida
Lenka Faltova
Paolo Arosio
item.description:
Authors: Katarzyna Makasewicz, Chiara Morelli, Tommaso Guida, Lenka Faltova, Paolo Arosio

Last/corresponding-author proxy: Lenka Faltova, Paolo Arosio

Score 5/10 C_MAYBE_UNCLASSIFIED

Why: Gemini failed; kept to avoid false negative

Abstract: Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-71939-2 RNA levels control phase separation of RNA-binding proteins in cells. Here, the authors show that high RNA concentrations suppress hnRNPA1 phase separation by inducing the formation of nano-sized protein-RNA clusters in the dilute phase, which convert to amyloid fibrils more slowly than condensates.

item.title: The current and future landscape of AI foundation models for cancer management
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72576-5

AI foundation models (FMs) are transforming cancer management and research. Here, we analyze the state-of-the-art FMs, discuss their impact on cancer management, and argue that the next generation of cancer AI FMs will be defined by multimodality, enhanced reasoning, maximized openness, and sustained human guidance.
item.dc:title: The current and future landscape of AI foundation models for cancer management
item.dc:creator: Chuang Niu, Ge Wang
Ge Wang
item.description:
Authors: Chuang Niu, Ge Wang

Last/corresponding-author proxy: Chuang Niu, Ge Wang

Score 5/10 C_MAYBE_UNCLASSIFIED

Why: Gemini failed; kept to avoid false negative

Abstract: Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72576-5 AI foundation models (FMs) are transforming cancer management and research. Here, we analyze the state-of-the-art FMs, discuss their impact on cancer management, and argue that the next generation of cancer AI FMs will be defined by multimodality, enhanced reasoning, maximized openness, and sustained human guidance.

item.title: Advancing environmental sustainability research and policy in sport
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72583-6

Sport holds profound global social influence, yet its systemic environmental footprint is often overlooked in sustainable development governance. Although environmental initiatives have proliferated in professional sport, many remain limited to public relations—fragmented, symbolic, and inadequate to tackle structural challenges. To facilitate a transition, a scientific framework is needed that offers coherent, verifiable pathways, bridging the gap between isolated sport initiatives and the broader systems of global environmental governance.
item.dc:title: Advancing environmental sustainability research and policy in sport
item.dc:creator: Yueting Zhang, Han Hao, Yikeranmu Yi Ming, Yaomin Zhang, Bing Shi
Han Hao
Yikeranmu Yi Ming
Yaomin Zhang
Bing Shi

Authors: Yueting Zhang, Han Hao, Yikeranmu Yi Ming, Yaomin Zhang, Bing Shi

Last/corresponding-author proxy: Yaomin Zhang, Bing Shi

Score 5/10 C_MAYBE_UNCLASSIFIED

Why: Gemini failed; kept to avoid false negative

Abstract: Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72583-6 Sport holds profound global social influence, yet its systemic environmental footprint is often overlooked in sustainable development governance. Although environmental initiatives have proliferated in professional sport, many remain limited to public relations—fragmented, symbolic, and inadequate to tackle structural challenges. To facilitate a transition, a scientific framework is needed that offers coherent, verifiable pathways, bridging the gap between isolated sport initiatives and the broader systems of global environmental governance.

item.title: Mechanochemical atom transfer radical polymerization unlocks functional methacrylate lifecycles of controlled synthesis, force-activated degradation, and depolymerization
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72346-3

Mechanochemical polymerisations enable solvent-free conditions, but applying them towards controlled radical polymerisations is a challenge. Here, the authors design methacrylate monomers with varying pendant groups for mechanochemical atom transfer radical polymerisation, and explore how these impact their polymerisation and depolymerisation kinetics.
item.dc:title: Mechanochemical atom transfer radical polymerization unlocks functional methacrylate lifecycles of controlled synthesis, force-activated degradation, and depolymerization
item.dc:creator: Gyeongjin Kwon, Daniel Yim, Soonhyuk Cha, Namhee Kim, Hyungjun Kim, Byeong-Su Kim

Score 5/10 C_MAYBE_UNCLASSIFIED

Why: Gemini failed; kept to avoid false negative

Abstract: Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72346-3 Mechanochemical polymerisations enable solvent-free conditions, but applying them towards controlled radical polymerisations is a challenge. Here, the authors design methacrylate monomers with varying pendant groups for mechanochemical atom transfer radical polymerisation, and explore how these impact their polymerisation and depolymerisation kinetics.

item.title: FMOPhore for hotspot identification and efficient fragment-to-lead growth strategies
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72384-x

The study introduces FMOPhore, a computational tool that identifies key binding site residues to improve drug design by guiding efficient fragment growth and lead optimization across diverse protein targets.
item.dc:title: FMOPhore for hotspot identification and efficient fragment-to-lead growth strategies
item.dc:creator: Peter E. G. F. Ibrahim, Simone Altmann, Ulrich Zachariae, David Horn, Ian H. Gilbert, Michael J. Bodkin
Simone Altmann
Ulrich Zachariae
David Horn

Michael J. Bodkin

Score 5/10 C_MAYBE_UNCLASSIFIED

Why: Gemini failed; kept to avoid false negative

Abstract: Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72384-x The study introduces FMOPhore, a computational tool that identifies key binding site residues to improve drug design by guiding efficient fragment growth and lead optimization across diverse protein targets.

item.title: Graph augmented transformers improve chemotherapy toxicity symptom extraction from clinical notes
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72347-2

Adverse events from chemotherapy are common; however, identifying related symptoms following these events from clinical documentation can be challenging. Here, the authors develop a natural language processing model to extract symptoms from clinical notes.
item.dc:title: Graph augmented transformers improve chemotherapy toxicity symptom extraction from clinical notes
item.dc:creator: Elia Saquand, Behzad Naderalvojoud, Maximilian Schuessler, et al.; last authors: Douglas W. Blayney, Tina Hernandez-Boussard

Score 5/10 C_MAYBE_UNCLASSIFIED

Why: Gemini failed; kept to avoid false negative

Abstract: Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72347-2 Adverse events from chemotherapy are common; however, identifying related symptoms following these events from clinical documentation can be challenging. Here, the authors develop a natural language processing model to extract symptoms from clinical notes.

item.title: Coiling of lugworm feces reveals universal mechanics for the shape of poo
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72566-7

Coiled animal excreta have fascinated naturalists since Darwin’s observations of earthworm castings, yet the physics are poorly understood. Authors study lugworm feces to provide a mechanical framework for fecal shape and how waste disposal in animals creates distinct buckling regimes.
item.dc:title: Coiling of lugworm feces reveals universal mechanics for the shape of poo
item.dc:creator: Mehdi Habibi, Neil M. Ribe, Daniel Bonn
Neil M. Ribe
Daniel Bonn
item.description:
Authors: Mehdi Habibi, Neil M. Ribe, Daniel Bonn

Last/corresponding-author proxy: Neil M. Ribe, Daniel Bonn

Score 5/10 C_MAYBE_UNCLASSIFIED

Why: Gemini failed; kept to avoid false negative

Abstract: Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72566-7 Coiled animal excreta have fascinated naturalists since Darwin’s observations of earthworm castings, yet the physics are poorly understood. Authors study lugworm feces to provide a mechanical framework for fecal shape and how waste disposal in animals creates distinct buckling regimes.

item.title: A high-strength radiative cooling biocomposite via hierarchical crystalline nanostructuring
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-72439-z

Biomass-based radiative cooling materials face trade-offs between performance, durability, and eco-friendly processes. Here, authors report a high-performance, recyclable cooling biocomposite directly fabricated from wood via an energy-saving method.
item.dc:title: A high-strength radiative cooling biocomposite via hierarchical crystalline nanostructuring
item.dc:creator: Xianxian Lin, Shuaiming He, Zhenkun Yang, et al.; last authors: Chaoji Chen, Yiqiang Wu

Here, authors report a high-performance, recyclable cooling biocomposite directly fabricated from wood via an energy-saving method.
